Fluid & Hemodynamic Disorders, Thromboembolic Diseases, and Shock

Combined Pathology Lecture Transes 6–8 + Laboratory Trans • Interactive master reviewer

⌕
Whole-lesson orientation

Overview

This tab maps the full sequence from fluid exchange to edema, vascular filling, hemorrhage, thrombosis, embolism, infarction, and shock. The lesson proper begins in Tab 2.

The master framework

Normal circulation
Fluid exchange + intact endothelium
Fluid imbalance
Edema
Abnormal blood volume
Hyperemia / congestion
Vessel rupture
Hemorrhage
Intravascular solid mass
Thrombosis
Detached traveling mass
Embolism
Ischemic necrosis
Infarction
Systemic hypoperfusion
Shock
Must knowThe lecture repeatedly connects morphology to mechanism: where the fluid or blood is located, whether a mass is attached to endothelium, whether tissue is pale or hemorrhagic, and whether injury is acute or chronic.

High-yield orientation table

DisorderCore mechanismSignature clue
EdemaExcess fluid in interstitium or body cavitiesIncreased hydrostatic pressure, decreased oncotic pressure, lymphatic obstruction, sodium retention, or inflammation
HyperemiaActive arteriolar dilationBright red, oxygenated blood
CongestionImpaired venous outflowDusky blue-red tissue; acute or chronic
HemorrhageExtravasation of RBCs after vessel ruptureBlood outside vessels
ThrombosisPathologic antemortem clot formationEndothelial attachment + Lines of Zahn
EmbolismDetached solid, liquid, or gas travels to a distant siteNo attachment at the site of lodgment
InfarctionIschemic necrosis after arterial or venous occlusionUsually wedge-shaped; coagulative necrosis except brain
ShockSystemic hypoperfusionLow effective circulating volume or cardiac output → widespread hypoxia

Numbers and thresholds to anchor

  • Water: about 60% of body weight in the lecture framework.
  • Exudate specific gravity: >1.020; transudate: <1.012.
  • Pulmonary thromboemboli: about 95% arise from deep veins of the lower extremities.
  • Thromboembolism: accounts for about 90% of embolic events.
  • PTE resolution: lecture states 60–80% resolve spontaneously.
  • Pulmonary infarction after PTE: approximately 5–10%.
  • Infarction: about 99% results from thrombotic or thromboembolic arterial occlusion.
  • AFE: lecture cites about 1 in 50,000 deliveries, 80% mortality, and permanent neurologic deficit in 85% of survivors.
Lec Trans 6–8

Five morphology questions to ask yourself

1. Where is the lesion?2. Intravascular or extravascular?3. Attached or detached?4. Acute or chronic?5. Pale, red, edematous, fibrotic, or necrotic?

These questions organize nearly every gross and microscopic plate in the lecture and laboratory trans.

Lesson proper begins here

Normal Fluid Homeostasis

Body-water compartments, capillary exchange, Starling-type forces, and the lymphatic safety valve.

Why fluid balance matters

Cell metabolism depends on an intact circulation for continuous delivery of oxygen, nutrients, hormones, electrolytes, and water, and for removal of metabolic waste and carbon dioxide. At the cellular level, exchange occurs among the intravascular, interstitial, cellular, and lymphatic spaces.

Lecture-specific warningAbnormalities of vascular permeability and hemostasis can cause cellular injury even when the main blood supply remains patent.
Trans 6, p.1

Distribution of body water

  • The lecture frames body weight as approximately 40% mineral/fat/protein and 60% water.
  • Most water is intracellular—about 2/3 of body water.
  • The remaining 1/3 is extracellular, with the interstitial compartment predominating and a smaller intravascular fraction.
Lecture approximationThe diagrams present two related frames: 40% body weight intracellular, 13% interstitial, 5% intravascular, and a simplified extracellular diagram labeled 95% interstitial / 5% intravascular. Preserve these as the lecture’s stated approximations.
Trans 6, pp.1–2

Normal capillary exchange

Fluid exchange occurs in the microcirculation. Functional junctional gaps between capillary endothelial cells permit movement of water while generally restricting larger molecules such as albumin.

Arteriolar end
Hydrostatic pressure > oncotic pressure
Interstitial space
Fluid filters outward
Venular end
Oncotic pressure > hydrostatic pressure
Lymphatics
Remove excess interstitial fluid
Normal outcomeThere is no net gain or loss of water in the interstitial space because reabsorption and lymphatic drainage balance filtration.
Trans 6, pp.2–3

Hydrostatic pressure

Hydrostatic pressure (HP) is the main force driving fluid from the intravascular space outward to the interstitium.

FactorLecture pathway
Cardiac output↑ CO → ↑ stroke volume → ↑ BP → ↑ HP
Vessel-wall elasticity↓ elasticity → ↑ BP → ↑ HP
Vascular tone↑ tone → ↑ BP → ↑ HP
Blood volume↑ volume → ↑ BP → ↑ HP
Trans 6, p.2

Oncotic pressure and lymphatics

Oncotic/osmotic pressure (OP) is the main force drawing fluid from the interstitium back into the circulation. The lecture uses serum albumin as its principal reflection.

  • Decreased OP means reduced reabsorptive capacity and favors edema.
  • Lecture examples: malnutrition, nephrotic syndrome, hypoalbuminemia, liver cirrhosis, protein-losing enteropathy.
  • Lymphatics return excess interstitial fluid to the venous circulation through the thoracic duct.
Trans 6, pp.2–3
Fluid accumulation

Edema

Classification, mechanisms, organ morphology, and clinical consequences.

Definition and distribution

Edema is an excessive accumulation of fluid in the interstitium and/or body cavities such as the pleural, peritoneal, pericardial, synovial, and periorbital spaces. It preferentially appears in loose tissues and dependent portions of the body.

LocalizedGeneralized / anasarcaInflammatory / exudativeNon-inflammatory / transudative
Trans 6, pp.3–5

Exudate versus transudate

FeatureExudateTransudate
Underlying eventIncreased vascular permeability from endothelial injury/inflammationHemodynamic imbalance: increased HP or decreased OP
ProteinHigh; protein-richLow; protein-poor
FibrinPresent / fibrin-richAbsent
CellsMany inflammatory cellsFew cells
Specific gravity>1.020<1.012
DistributionUsually localizedOften generalized
Exam trapInflammatory edema = exudate. Pressure-gradient edema = transudate.
Trans 6, p.4

Localized and generalized forms

  • Hydropericardium: pericardial sac.
  • Hydrothorax / pleural effusion: pleural cavity. The lecture states the normal thoracic cavity contains about 100 mL of clear watery fluid.
  • Hydroperitoneum / ascites: peritoneal cavity.
  • Bullae and vesicles: dermal interstitial fluid; vesicles are smaller.
  • Anasarca: widespread whole-body edema.
  • Hydrops fetalis: diffuse fetal edema with swollen, red, shiny skin.
Trans 6, pp.4–5

Five pathophysiologic categories

  1. Increased hydrostatic pressure
  2. Decreased plasma oncotic pressure
  3. Lymphatic obstruction
  4. Sodium and water retention
  5. Inflammation / increased vascular permeability
If you see X, think YCHF → increased HP. Nephrotic syndrome or malnutrition → decreased OP. Post-lymph-node dissection or filariasis → lymphatic obstruction. Allergy/insect bite → inflammatory permeability.
Trans 6, p.6

Edema in congestive heart failure

Weak/failing LV
↓ cardiac output + backflow
↑ LA and pulmonary blood volume
↑ hydrostatic pressure
Pulmonary edema

Reduced effective circulating volume also activates RAAS, vasopressin, and the sympathetic nervous system, producing renal sodium and water retention, increased plasma volume, and further elevation of hydrostatic pressure.

Must knowIn the lecture, edema is a prominent manifestation of heart failure and may be localized to the lungs or generalized depending on severity.
Trans 6, p.6

Cirrhosis and nephrotic syndrome

Cirrhosis

Portal hypertension raises hydrostatic pressure and produces ascites. Loss of functioning hepatocytes reduces albumin synthesis, causing hypoalbuminemia and generalized edema. Splanchnic vasodilation and reduced effective arterial blood volume activate baroreceptors and sodium/water retention.

Nephrotic syndrome

Glomerular damage permits urinary loss of albumin (proteinuria) → hypoalbuminemia → decreased OP. An intrinsic defect in sodium/water excretion and reduced GFR also contribute. RAAS-mediated retention may lead to hypervolemia and increased HP.

Trans 6, pp.6–7

Lymphatic obstruction and sodium retention

Lymphatic obstruction reduces removal of interstitial water and creates localized lymphedema. Lecture causes include filariasis (Wuchereria bancrofti), radiation, radical mastectomy, and lymph-node dissection. Severe lower-extremity filarial lymphedema is described as elephantiasis.

Sodium retention expands intravascular volume, increases HP, and dilutes colloid osmotic pressure. It occurs with decreased renal perfusion/function, increased salt intake, increased tubular reabsorption, and changes in ADH or RAAS. The lecture’s cue is simple: water follows sodium.

Trans 6, pp.7–8

Inflammatory edema

Endothelial injury increases permeability, allowing leakage of water, protein, fibrin, and inflammatory cells. Lecture examples include laryngeal edema from allergic/hypersensitivity reactions, insect bites, bullae, wheals, and edema associated with acute/chronic inflammation and granulation tissue.

Clinical warning in the lectureLaryngeal edema can obstruct the airway and is treated as a medical emergency.
Trans 6, pp.8–9

Morphology of edema

  • Gross: organ enlargement and increased weight; moist, wet, glistening surfaces; shiny mucosa; dependent pitting; periorbital or subcutaneous swelling.
  • Histology: poorly staining extracellular material; loosening and separation of extracellular matrix; scattered, widely spaced cells.
  • Water stains poorly on H&E and may appear light pink to colorless.
Clinical cuePitting edema leaves an indentation after pressure because fluid has accumulated in soft tissue.
Trans 6, pp.9–10

Pulmonary edema

Fluid fills alveolar spaces and mechanically interferes with gas exchange. Patients may have dyspnea, orthopnea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea.

  • Gross: swollen, heavy, moist, reddish, less crepitant lung; frothy/bubbly fluid may escape when the cut surface is pressed.
  • Microscopy: distended alveoli containing pale pink, relatively acellular proteinaceous transudate with congested septal capillaries.
Trans 6, pp.10–12

Cerebral edema

The brain is enclosed in a rigid skull, so swelling raises intracranial pressure and may cause brain herniation. The lecture specifically emphasizes tonsillar herniation through the foramen magnum, which can compress vital cardiorespiratory centers.

  • Associated conditions: brain tumor, infection, hypertension, trauma, metabolic encephalopathy.
  • Gross: heavy, swollen brain; narrowed sulci; widened/flattened gyri; possible hemispheric asymmetry with expanded white matter.
  • Clinical course: stupor, coma, death.
Trans 6, p.12
Blood volume and vessel rupture

Hyperemia, Congestion, and Hemorrhage

Differentiate active inflow, passive outflow obstruction, and RBC extravasation.

Hyperemia versus congestion

FeatureHyperemiaCongestion
ProcessActivePassive
MechanismArteriolar dilation from neurohumoral/SNS or vasoactive stimulationImpaired venous drainage or localized venous obstruction
BloodMore oxygenated bloodRetained deoxygenated blood
ColorBright red/pink, erythematousDusky red-blue, cyanotic
ExamplesBlushing, exercise, acute inflammationCHF, venous obstruction
TimingLecture states it is acuteMay be acute or chronic
Do not confuseHemorrhage differs from both because RBCs have left the vascular space.
Trans 6, pp.13,17

Acute pulmonary congestion

  • May follow an acute inflammatory or hemodynamic event.
  • Gross: heavy, dark red to violaceous lung.
  • Microscopy: alveolar septal capillaries are dilated, engorged, and packed with RBCs.
  • Often accompanied by edema and alveolar hemorrhage.
Trans 6, p.14; Lab pp.9–11

Chronic passive congestion of the lung

Chronic LV failure
Back pressure in LA/pulmonary vessels
Septal capillary congestion + edema
Microhemorrhage
Hemosiderin-laden macrophages

Long-standing congestion produces thickened, fibrotic alveolar septa and heart failure cells. Grossly, the lung becomes heavy, firm, and brown—brown induration. A long-term complication is pulmonary hypertension.

Trans 6, p.14; Lab pp.12–22

Hepatic and splenic congestion

Acute hepatic congestion

Seen in acute right ventricular failure. Congestion is chiefly centrilobular, around the central vein; severe cases may show centrilobular hepatocyte necrosis and hemorrhage while periportal areas are relatively spared.

Chronic passive congestion

The liver develops a mottled nutmeg appearance: dark congested centrilobular zones alternate with lighter periportal zones. Long-standing congestion can cause fibrosis and cardiac cirrhosis. In the spleen, chronic congestion produces congestive splenomegaly with an enlarged blood-engorged parenchyma and thickened fibrotic capsule.

Trans 6, pp.14–16

Hemorrhage: definition, types, and significance

Hemorrhage is extravasation of RBCs secondary to rupture of a blood vessel.

ClassificationTermLecture description
By siteHemothoraxBlood in pleural/thoracic cavity; may impair respiration
HemopericardiumBlood in pericardial cavity; may cause cardiac tamponade
HemoperitoneumBlood in peritoneal cavity
HemarthrosisBlood in a synovial joint
By sizePetechiaePinpoint, usually 1–2 mm; skin, mucosa, visceral surfaces
PurpuraMore than 3 mm; flat dark-red lesions
EcchymosisIrregular, map-like 1–2 cm lesions; bruises
HematomaLarge localized pool of extravasated blood

Causes

Trauma/laceration, atherosclerotic vessel-wall injury, inflammation, neoplastic erosion, and hemorrhagic diathesis.

Clinical effect

Severity depends on volume, rate, and site. The lecture states that loss of more than one-third or about 20% of circulating volume can produce hemorrhagic/hypovolemic shock. Rapid loss is worse because compensation has no time to act. Small-volume bleeding can be fatal in the brain, lungs, or pericardium.

Trans 6, p.17
Physiologic clot control

Normal Hemostasis

How an intact system rapidly seals vascular injury while limiting unnecessary thrombosis.

Core definition and components

Hemostasis is the process by which blood clots form at sites of vascular injury. It simultaneously maintains blood in a fluid, clot-free state and forms a hemostatic plug when a vessel is damaged.

Blood vessel / endotheliumPlateletsCoagulation systemAntithrombotic controls
Key distinctionA hemostatic plug is a normal, protective response. A thrombus is the pathologic counterpart formed intravascularly in a living person.
Trans 7, p.3

Four-event sequence

  1. Transient arterial vasoconstriction
  2. Primary hemostatic plug—platelet adhesion, activation, secretion, aggregation, contraction
  3. Secondary hemostatic plug—coagulation cascade and fibrin stabilization
  4. Antithrombotic regulation—limit growth, stabilize or dissolve clot
Essential sequenceVasoconstriction → primary platelet plug → fibrin-stabilized secondary plug → control/resorption.
Trans 7, pp.3–7

1. Vasoconstriction and endothelial contribution

Vascular injury triggers transient SNS- and endothelin-mediated vasoconstriction. This reduces blood flow, disrupts laminar flow, and permits platelets and clotting factors to contact the injured area.

Prothrombotic endothelial actions

  • vWF supports platelet binding.
  • Tissue factor / thromboplastin activates the extrinsic pathway.
  • Plasminogen activator inhibitors oppose fibrinolysis.

Antithrombotic endothelial actions

  • Platelet inhibitors: prostacyclin, ADPase, nitric oxide.
  • Anticoagulants: thrombomodulin, heparin-like molecules.
  • Fibrinolysis: t-PA.
Trans 7, p.4

2. Primary hemostasis: platelet plug

Platelets are disc-shaped, anucleate cells derived from megakaryocytes. Their four contributions are adhesion, secretion, aggregation, and contraction.

Endothelial injury exposes collagen
GPIb binds via vWF
Shape change + granule release
ADP/TXA₂ recruit platelets
Temporary platelet plug
Do not confusevWF deficiency and absence of GPIb cause defective adhesion in the examples cited by the lecture.
Trans 7, pp.4–5

Platelet granules and activation

GranuleLecture contentsRole
Dense bodiesADP, Ca²⁺, serotonin, histamine, epinephrineADP activates/recruits platelets; calcium supports coagulation
Alpha granulesFibrinogen, fibronectin, PDGF, PF4, thrombospondinCoagulation, repair, and platelet response

Activated platelets change from smooth discs to spiky “sea-urchin” forms, increasing surface area and exposing phospholipid surfaces that help assemble coagulation-factor complexes.

Trans 7, p.5

3. Secondary hemostasis: fibrin clot

The coagulation system converts the unstable platelet plug into a stable secondary plug.

  • Extrinsic pathway: triggered by endothelial tissue factor/thromboplastin.
  • Intrinsic pathway: contact with an abnormal surface activates Factor XII, followed by XI, IX, VIII, and X with calcium and platelet factor 3.
  • Common pathway: Factor X activation → prothrombin (II) to thrombin (IIa) → fibrinogen to fibrin → Factor XIII cross-linking/polymerization.
Final productInsoluble fibrin acts as the cement that stabilizes the platelet aggregate.
Trans 7, p.6

4. Anticlotting mechanisms

ControlLecture mechanism
Clotting-factor depletion and hepatic clearanceLimits accumulation of activated factors and helps keep blood fluid
Fibrinolysist-PA/urokinase-type activators convert plasminogen to plasmin, which cleaves fibrin
Protease inhibitorsAntithrombin III and other inhibitors oppose thrombin/coagulation factors
Antiplatelet endothelial productsProstacyclin, nitric oxide, and ADPase limit platelet aggregation

The balance between prothrombotic and antithrombotic activities determines whether a clot propagates or dissolves.

Trans 7, pp.6–7
Pathologic intravascular clotting

Thrombosis and Virchow’s Triad

Definition, predisposing mechanisms, and the distinction from postmortem clot.

Definition

Thrombosis is formation of a solid clotted mass (thrombus) from blood elements—vessel wall, platelets, and clotting factors—intravascularly in a living person. It is also called an antemortem clot.

  • Clotting refers to coagulation in vitro, such as in a test tube.
  • A thrombus may obstruct blood flow, cause ischemia/infarction, or detach to form a thromboembolus.
Morphologic proof of antemortem formationEndothelial attachment and Lines of Zahn.
Trans 7, pp.1–3

Antemortem versus postmortem clot

FeatureAntemortem clot / thrombusPostmortem clot
Gross surfaceDry, rough, granular; may not fit vessel contourMoist, smooth, jelly-like; forms a perfect cast
AttachmentAttached to injured endotheliumNo attachment
EndotheliumDamaged/injuredNo endothelial damage
CompositionPlatelets, fibrin/clotting factors, RBCs, WBCsMainly fibrin with entrapped RBCs and WBCs
Lines of ZahnPresent; laminatedAbsent; non-laminated
SettingLiving personAfter death or after phlebotomy
Classic namesThrombusChicken-fat and currant-jelly clot
Trans 7, p.2; Lab pp.43–50

Virchow’s triad

Thrombus formation and propagation arise from abnormalities in:

  1. Vessel wall / endothelial injury
  2. Blood flow—stasis or turbulence
  3. Blood components—hypercoagulability

The contemporary view in the lecture also considers inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, platelet function, coagulation, fibrinolysis, and hormonal factors. Endothelial dysfunction is identified as the most important component.

Trans 7, p.8

1. Endothelial injury

Injury exposes subendothelial collagen, promotes platelet adhesion, and shifts the endothelial balance toward thrombosis.

  • Atherosclerosis, hyperlipidemia, hypercholesterolemia
  • Hypertension—mechanical trauma from elevated pressure
  • Acute myocardial infarction and vasculitis
  • Cigarette smoking—nicotine is described as endotheliotoxic
Trans 7, p.8

2. Abnormal blood flow

Turbulence may injure/activate endothelium, disrupt laminar flow, bring platelets to the wall, and prevent dilution/washout of activated factors. Stasis permits margination and is especially important in venous thrombosis.

  • Turbulence examples: atherosclerotic plaque, valvular stenosis/regurgitation, vascular obstruction.
  • Stasis examples: aneurysm, post-MI mural thrombus, rheumatic mitral stenosis, immobilization.
Trans 7, pp.8–9

3. Hypercoagulability

Thrombophilia is an increased predisposition to venous thrombosis and may be genetic or acquired.

  • Genetic: Factor V mutation; increased factors VIII, IX, XI, or fibrinogen.
  • Acquired: prolonged bed rest/immobilization, disseminated cancer, prosthetic valves, DIC, oral contraceptives.
  • Other lecture associations: malignancy, APAS, nephrotic syndrome, reproductive hormones.
Trans 7, p.9
Gross, microscopic, and outcomes

Thrombus Morphology and DIC

Recognize the lesion, predict its behavior, and understand consumption coagulopathy.

General morphology and Lines of Zahn

Thrombi may form in arteries, veins, cardiac chambers, or microcirculation. Their composition reflects the rate of blood flow.

  • Endothelial attachment results from platelet adhesion to an injured wall.
  • Lines of Zahn are alternating pale platelet/fibrin laminations and darker RBC/WBC-rich layers.
  • They are most apparent in thrombi formed in flowing blood, especially the heart and aorta.
Microscopy cuePale paucicellular layer = platelets + fibrin. Dark cellular layer = RBCs + WBCs.
Trans 7, pp.3,9–10

Major thrombus types

TypeSite and morphology
Mural / non-occlusiveLarge chambers such as ventricles, atria, or aorta; may overlie MI, myocarditis, or aneurysm
VegetationAttached to cardiac valves; lecture describes sterile thrombotic vegetations without microbial colonies
Arterial / white / conglutinationPlatelet- and fibrin-rich, relatively RBC-poor; often occlusive and ischemic
Venous / red / phlebothrombosisRBC-rich, formed in slow venous flow/stasis; prone to propagation and fragmentation
MicrocirculatoryCapillaries, arterioles, venules; characteristic of DIC
Trans 7, pp.10–12

Arterial versus venous thrombus

FeatureArterialVenous
Typical driverEndothelial injury / turbulenceStasis
GrowthRetrogradeForward propagation
Main consequenceOcclusion → ischemia/infarctionOcclusion and fragmentation/embolization
AppearanceGray-white / paleDark red
CompositionMore platelets and fibrin; fewer RBCsMore RBCs and leukocytes
Trans 7, p.11

Fates of a thrombus

Propagation

Further accumulation of platelets and fibrin enlarges the thrombus, increasing occlusion and ischemia.

Lysis / resolution

Plasmin-mediated fibrinolysis dissolves the thrombus. Small, early thrombi are most likely to resolve completely.

Embolization

A friable fragment detaches, travels, and lodges at a distant site.

Organization / recanalization

Phagocytes digest clot; capillaries and fibroblasts grow inward; the thrombus becomes fibrovascular scar incorporated into the wall. New channels may restore some flow.

Late morphologyA fibrosed thrombus may hyalinize and calcify, forming a phlebolith.
Trans 7, pp.13–14

Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)

DIC is a special thromboembolic disorder also called defibrination syndrome, microcirculatory thrombosis, or consumption coagulopathy. It may be acute, subacute, or chronic and occurs secondary to another clinical disorder.

Systemic thrombin activation
Widespread microthrombi
Ischemia and microinfarcts
Consumption of clotting factors
Paradoxical hemorrhage
Shock / poor outcome

Clinical associations in the lecture

Sepsis, burns, snake bites, shock, abruptio placentae, amniotic fluid embolism, retained dead fetus, and carcinomatosis.

Morphology

  • Multiple microthrombi in brain, heart, lungs, kidneys, and adrenals.
  • Secondary ischemic/hypoxic tissue injury.
  • Multiple hemorrhages.
High-risk patternThe lecture describes DIC as having a very poor outcome, with widespread thrombosis followed by depletion of factors and uncontrolled bleeding.
Trans 7, pp.14–15; Trans 8, p.6
A mass that travels

Embolism

Thromboembolism, systemic embolism, gas, amniotic fluid, fat, and other embolic material.

Definition and classification

Embolism is the process by which a detached intravascular solid, liquid, or gaseous mass is carried through the bloodstream to a site distant from its origin, partially or completely obstructing the cardiovascular system.

Physical formLecture examples
SolidDetached thrombus, atheromatous fragments, tumor-cell clumps, tissue fragments
LiquidAmniotic fluid
GasAir or nitrogen bubbles

Emboli may also be bland (sterile) or septic (infected). About 90% are thromboemboli.

Trans 8, p.1

Pulmonary thromboembolism (PTE)

PTE is the most common and fatal form of venous thromboembolism. Unlike rare in-situ pulmonary thrombosis, the thrombus originates elsewhere—usually the lower extremity deep veins—and travels to the pulmonary arterial tree.

Popliteal / femoral / iliac DVT
Inferior vena cava
Right atrium
Right ventricle
Pulmonary arteries
  • Lecture states 95% arise from deep veins of the lower extremities.
  • Risk settings: hospitalized, bedridden, prolonged immobilization, poor cardiovascular status, carcinoma, old age, late pregnancy, postpartum period, contraceptive-pill use.
  • Morphology: saddle embolus, multiple small emboli, paradoxical embolus.
Histology cueAn embolus may retain Lines of Zahn but lacks endothelial attachment at the site of lodgment because it formed elsewhere.
Trans 8, pp.1–2

Clinical sequelae of PTE

OutcomeMechanism / presentation
Acute RV failure (acute cor pulmonale)Large saddle embolus or numerous emboli obstruct pulmonary circulation → sudden elevation of PA and RV pressure; platelet vasoconstrictors/reflex vasoconstriction may contribute
Sudden cardiac deathMassive embolism may cause instantaneous death; delayed cases may resemble MI with chest pain, dyspnea, and shock
Pulmonary hypertensionAcute or chronic obstruction; organized recurrent small emboli can produce chronic pulmonary hypertension
Resolution / organizationLecture states 60–80% resolve via fibrinolysis; bronchial circulation may keep patients clinically silent
Pulmonary infarctionSmall-branch occlusion; chest pain from fibrinous pleuritis, hemoptysis, dyspnea
Pulmonary hemorrhageTerminal branch obstruction; hemoptysis and dyspnea; chest pain less common with central hemorrhage
Trans 8, pp.2–3

Systemic embolism

Systemic/arterial emboli usually arise from the left side of the heart, especially mural thrombi in a diseased left ventricle or dilated left atrium.

  • Lecture sources: about 80% mural thrombus; LV after acute MI; LA dilation in rheumatic heart disease; aortic aneurysm and valves. About 10–15% have no ascertained source.
  • Predisposing cardiac diseases: MI, cardiomyopathy, rheumatic or congenital heart disease, infective endocarditis, prosthetic valves.
  • Lodgment: lower extremities 75%, brain 10%, remainder in viscera and upper extremities.
Must knowSystemic emboli are described as almost always occlusive and therefore tend to produce infarction.
Trans 8, pp.3–4

Gas embolism and decompression sickness

Air, nitrogen, or other gas bubbles may coalesce into frothy masses that obstruct vascular flow. The lecture states a clinically significant air volume of about 100–150 mL.

Venous air embolism

Head/neck surgery with jugular entry, obstetric trauma, positive-pressure infusions, angiography.

Arterial air embolism

Cardiothoracic surgery/trauma, thoracentesis, lung rupture or penetrating wounds, arteriography, or paradoxical passage through a patent foramen ovale/pulmonary AV shunt.

Decompression sickness

Rapid pressure change releases dissolved gases—mainly nitrogen—into bubbles. Acute disease causes “the bends,” “the chokes,” and cerebral effects. Chronic Caisson disease produces avascular necrosis of tibia/femoral heads/humerus, fractures, muscle atrophy, neurologic deficits, pulmonary changes, and skin manifestations.

Trans 8, pp.4–5

Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)

AFE is a serious, unpredictable, and unpreventable cause of maternal mortality during labor or the immediate postpartum period. Amniotic material enters uterine veins through myometrial/endocervical tears or is forced into uterine sinusoids by vigorous contractions, then reaches the right heart and lungs.

  • Embolic components: fetal squames, vernix caseosa, lanugo hair, meconium bile, mucus.
  • Lecture incidence: 1 in 50,000 deliveries, mortality about 80%, permanent neurologic deficit in 85% of survivors.
  • Complications: DIC, diffuse alveolar damage, pulmonary edema, ARDS.
Histology cueFetal squames and amniotic debris plug pulmonary microvessels, with congestion and focal atelectasis.
Trans 8, pp.5–6

Fat embolism

Fat and fatty debris obstruct arterioles and capillaries. The most common traumatic setting is long-bone fracture, where marrow fat enters venous blood and lodges in the lungs. Most cases are asymptomatic; about 10% develop fat embolism syndrome with pulmonary insufficiency, neurologic symptoms, anemia, rash, and thrombocytopenia; the lecture cites a 10% fatality rate.

Four mechanisms

  1. Mechanical obstruction by released fat globules
  2. Emulsion instability with plasma-lipid aggregation in nontraumatic cases
  3. Intravascular coagulation with clotting-factor release and DIC
  4. Toxic endothelial injury from free fatty acids → permeability and pulmonary edema

On routine H&E, dissolved fat appears as round empty intravascular spaces; Sudan or Oil Red O stains demonstrate fat. Pulmonary infarction is usually not a feature because globules are small.

Trans 8, pp.6–7

Other emboli

Atheroembolism

Fragments of dislodged atherosclerotic material.

Tumor embolism

Solid clusters of tumor cells traveling within vessels from a primary tumor.

Tissue embolism

Detached tissue fragments carried in the circulation.

Trans 8, p.7
Ischemic necrosis

Infarction

Etiology, general morphology, white versus red infarcts, septic versus bland, and organ patterns.

Definition and causes

Infarction is an area of ischemic necrosis of an organ or tissue caused by arterial or venous occlusion. The lecture states that about 99% results from thrombotic or thromboembolic arterial occlusion.

Etiologies in the lecture

  • Thrombotic, embolic, or thromboembolic occlusion
  • Extrinsic compression
  • Twisting of vessels
  • Trapping of a viscus
  • Vasospasm
Trans 8, pp.7–8

General morphology

  • Often wedge-shaped: apex points toward the occluded vessel; broad base lies at the organ surface.
  • Arterial infarcts are generally pale/anemic; venous infarcts are generally hemorrhagic/red.
  • An inflammatory reaction develops at the periphery.
  • Most organs undergo coagulative necrosis.
  • The brain undergoes liquefactive necrosis with encephalomalacia.
  • Older infarcts become fibrous scars and may show dystrophic calcification.
Trans 8, p.8

White versus red infarct

White / anemicRed / hemorrhagic
Arterial occlusionVenous occlusion or arterial/venous occlusion in selected settings
Solid organs with end-arterial supplyLoose/spongy tissues such as lung
Heart, kidney, spleenOrgans with dual circulation, previously congested tissue, or reperfused infarcts
Pale, sharply demarcated wedgeDark red because RBCs accumulate in necrotic tissue
Hemorrhagic-infarct settingsVenous occlusion, loose tissue, dual blood supply, previous congestion, and revascularization.
Trans 8, pp.8–10

Septic versus bland infarct

  • Septic infarct: infected embolus or secondary infection of necrotic tissue; may form an abscess and show acute inflammation.
  • Bland infarct: sterile ischemic necrosis.
Do not confuse“Red versus white” describes the amount of hemorrhage. “Septic versus bland” describes whether infection is present.
Trans 8, p.10

Factors affecting infarct development

  1. Nature of vascular supply: dual blood supply protects against infarction but may make an infarct hemorrhagic.
  2. Rate of occlusion: slow occlusion may allow collateral circulation; sudden occlusion is more damaging.
  3. Tissue vulnerability to hypoxia: neurons are highly susceptible; fibroblasts are more resistant.
  4. Oxygen content of blood: anemia or hypoxemia worsens ischemic injury.
Trans 8, p.10

Organ examples

Organ / lesionLecture pattern
Acute myocardial infarctionCoronary thrombotic occlusion, often LAD in the case; pale infarct and coagulative necrosis of cardiac myocytes
Renal infarctPale triangular lesion with base under capsule and apex toward medulla; ghost outlines of tubules/glomeruli
Splenic infarctBland pale wedge-shaped subcapsular lesion; coagulative necrosis
Pulmonary infarctHemorrhagic because lung is loose and has pulmonary + bronchial circulation
Cerebral infarctLiquefactive necrosis / encephalomalacia
GangreneDistal-limb infarction with coagulative necrosis plus liquefactive change from bacterial infection/putrefaction; black gross appearance
Trans 8, pp.9–12

Splenic infarct morphology

  • Gross: enlarged spleen; bland, pale wedge with broad edge against capsule; brown, firm background; occasional capsular fibrin.
  • Histology: sharp line of demarcation; pink homogeneous, less cellular infarct; inflammatory cells at the margin.
  • Coagulative necrosis: architecture and cell outlines persist temporarily, with increased eosinophilia and loss of nuclei.
  • Lecture/lab sources include cardiac thromboemboli from atrial fibrillation, post-MI mural thrombus, and infective endocarditis.
Lab Trans, pp.23–28
Systemic hypoperfusion

Shock

Types, shared final pathway, stages, and organ morphology.

Definition and shared consequence

Shock is systemic hypoperfusion secondary to reduced cardiac output or reduced effective circulating blood volume. It produces inadequate perfusion, cellular hypoxia/anoxia, impaired metabolism, and—if uncompensated—cell and organ death.

↓ venous return or pump output
↓ effective circulating volume / CO
↓ tissue perfusion
Cellular hypoxia
Metabolic dysfunction
Organ failure
Trans 8, p.12

Types of shock

TypePrimary eventLecture examples / mechanism
CardiogenicMyocardial pump failure without actual blood-volume lossMassive MI / myocardial standstill → sudden fall in CO
Hypovolemic / hemorrhagicReduced blood or plasma volumeHemorrhage from trauma; plasma/water loss from severe diarrhea/dehydration
SepticBacteremia/septicemia and endotoxin-mediated vasodilationUsually gram-negative bacteria in lecture; peripheral pooling → ↓ venous return and CO
NeurogenicNeurohumoral/SNS-mediated peripheral vasodilationAnesthetic-induced or spinal-cord injury examples
AnaphylacticIgE-mediated hypersensitivity with peripheral vasodilationSame broad low-return mechanism as septic/neurogenic in the lecture framework
Trans 8, pp.12–13

Stage 1: nonprogressive

Compensated, reversible. BP and CO are maintained by reflex mechanisms to preserve vital-organ perfusion.

  • Baroreceptor responses
  • Catecholamines / SNS
  • ADH and RAAS
  • Peripheral vasoconstriction

Clinical signs: tachycardia, skin pallor, vasoconstriction, renal fluid conservation.

Trans 8, p.13

Stage 2: progressive

The underlying cause persists; BP and CO decline. Cells shift from aerobic metabolism to anaerobic glycolysis, producing lactic acid and metabolic acidosis.

Hypoperfusion
Acidosis
↓ vasomotor response
Vasodilation / pooling
Further ↓ CO

Clinical signs: worsening hypotension, oliguria, cerebral depression.

Trans 8, p.13

Stage 3: irreversible

Compensatory mechanisms fail and severe hypoxia causes extensive cellular injury.

  • Marked fall in BP and CO
  • Renal shutdown and tubular necrosis
  • Worsening metabolic acidosis
  • Coma and death
  • Multiple-organ failure
Trans 8, p.13

Morphology of shock

OrganLecture morphology
BrainIschemic encephalopathy with neuronal death
HeartContraction-band and subendocardial necrosis
KidneyAcute tubular necrosis
LungARDS / “shock lung” with pulmonary edema, pneumocyte necrosis, and hyaline membranes
AdrenalsCortical-cell lipid depletion
GITHemorrhagic enteropathy
LiverFatty change and central hemorrhagic necrosis
Final common pathwayRegardless of the initiating type, persistent shock produces a vicious cycle of hypoperfusion, hypoxia, acidosis, vasomotor failure, and organ dysfunction.
Trans 8, p.13
Gross and microscopic correlation

Laboratory Morphology

Integrated specimen descriptions from the Pathology Laboratory trans, organized as recognition plates rather than practice questions.

Congested appendix / acute appendicitis

The laboratory case describes an 11-year-old with fever, RLQ rebound tenderness, nausea, and vomiting.

  • Gross: swollen, enlarged, reddish appendix with prominent serosal vessels; serosa may be covered by thick creamy fibrinopurulent exudate.
  • Normal layers: serosa, muscularis (inner circular/outer longitudinal smooth muscle), submucosa with lymphoid tissue, mucosa with simple columnar epithelium and intestinal glands.
  • Pathologic histology: congested serosal vessels; neutrophils invading muscularis; distended submucosal vessels filled with RBCs; increased lymphoid tissue; lumen filled with exudate.
Recognition cueNeutrophils in the muscularis plus vascular congestion and luminal exudate support acute appendicitis in the lab plate.
Lab Trans, pp.4–8

Acute pulmonary congestion

  • Gross: distended lung; cut surface may extrude frothy material when pressed; normal lung should be pink and shiny.
  • Normal histology: thin delicate alveolar walls; well-aerated alveoli.
  • Pathologic histology: alveoli contain smooth to slightly floccular pink material; capillaries are congested with many RBCs.
  • Lab association: heart failure, inflammation, or acute volume overload.
Lab Trans, pp.9–11

Chronic passive pulmonary congestion

  • Gross: enlarged, heavy, solid, dark reddish-brown lung with frothy fluid; “brown induration.”
  • Microscopy: thickened fibrotic alveolar septa; congested capillaries; transudate in alveolar spaces; hemosiderin-laden macrophages.
  • Heart failure cells: macrophages phagocytose extravasated RBC breakdown products and accumulate hemosiderin.
Lab Trans, pp.12–22, 51–54

Splenic infarction

  • Gross: pale, well-demarcated, wedge/pyramidal, subcapsular lesion with broad base at capsule.
  • Normal spleen landmarks: capsule and trabeculae; white pulp with central arteries; red pulp with venous sinuses and cords of Billroth.
  • Histology: line of demarcation, homogeneous eosinophilic less-cellular tissue, loss of nuclei, inflammatory cells at the margin.
Lab Trans, pp.23–28

Pulmonary thromboembolism specimen

  • Long tubular thrombus approximating the form of leg veins.
  • A saddle embolus lodges at the bifurcation of the pulmonary trunk.
  • Predisposing sequence: DVT + immobilization/hypercoagulability/postoperative state → embolization to pulmonary arterial system.
  • May completely occlude pulmonary flow, cause acute cor pulmonale, and produce sudden death.
  • Histologic plate may show luminal occlusion, Lines of Zahn, and adjacent infarcted parenchyma.
Lab Trans, pp.29–30

Clot identification checklist

Attached?Rough or smooth?Lines of Zahn?Perfect vascular cast?
  • Antemortem: attached, rough/granular, laminated.
  • Postmortem: nonadherent, smooth/jelly-like, molded to vessel.
  • Chicken-fat layer: upper yellow portion after gravity separation.
  • Currant-jelly layer: dark red RBC-rich dependent portion.
Lab Trans, pp.43–50

One-line slide recognition

SlideMost useful clues
Acute appendicitisProminent congested vessels + neutrophils through muscularis + fibrinopurulent exudate
Acute pulmonary congestionSeptal capillaries packed with RBCs + pale pink edema fluid
Chronic pulmonary congestionFibrotic septa + hemosiderin-laden macrophages
Splenic infarctPale wedge + coagulative necrosis + line of demarcation
PTEEmbolic mass in pulmonary artery; may show Lines of Zahn without local endothelial attachment
Antemortem clotEndothelial attachment + alternating laminations
Common confusions

Comparisons

Side-by-side distinctions drawn directly from the lecture and laboratory transes.

Edema, congestion, and hemorrhage

FeatureEdemaCongestionHemorrhage
What accumulates?Fluid/waterBlood within vesselsRBCs outside vessels
Main siteInterstitium or body cavityVenous/capillary vascular bedExtravascular tissue/cavity
Color cluePale pink or poorly staining fluidDusky red-blueRed collections / pigment breakdown
Key mechanismHP, OP, lymphatic, sodium, permeability imbalanceImpaired venous outflowVessel rupture

Primary versus secondary hemostasis

FeaturePrimary hemostasisSecondary hemostasis
Main actorPlateletsCoagulation factors
Key sequenceAdhesion → activation/secretion → aggregation → contractionIntrinsic/extrinsic pathways → Factor X → thrombin → fibrin
Key bridge/productvWF bridges collagen to GPIbInsoluble cross-linked fibrin
PlugTemporary, unstable platelet plugStable fibrin-cemented plug

Thrombus, embolus, infarct

TermDefinitionSignature morphologyMajor consequence
ThrombusSolid clotted mass formed intravascularly in a living personAttached to endothelium; Lines of ZahnOcclusion or embolization
EmbolusDetached mass carried to distant siteNo local attachment; may retain thrombus laminationsDistant vascular obstruction
InfarctArea of ischemic necrosis after occlusionOften wedge-shaped; coagulative necrosis except brainIrreversible tissue death

Acute versus chronic pulmonary congestion

AcuteChronic
Capillary engorgementPersistent capillary engorgement
Alveolar edema / hemorrhageRepeated microhemorrhage
Minimal fibrosisThickened fibrotic septa
No established heart failure cellsHemosiderin-laden macrophages

White versus red infarct

WhiteRed
Solid organ, end-arterial circulationLoose tissue, dual circulation, venous occlusion, previous congestion, reperfusion
Heart, kidney, spleenLung and bowel examples
Pale / anemicHemorrhagic / dark red

Shock stages

StageHemodynamicsMetabolismClinical/morphologic direction
NonprogressiveBP/CO maintained by reflexesCompensation preserves vital organsTachycardia, pallor, vasoconstriction; reversible
ProgressiveBP/CO fall; microcirculatory poolingAnaerobic glycolysis → lactic acidosisHypotension, oliguria, cerebral depression
IrreversibleSevere circulatory collapseProfound hypoxia and acidosisATN, coma, multiple-organ failure, death
Final rapid review

Cram Sheet

The true final tab: dense, lecture-supported recall points without practice questions.

Fluid and edema

  • HP pushes out; OP pulls in; lymphatics drain excess.
  • Normal: arteriolar HP > OP; venular OP > HP; no net interstitial gain.
  • Edema mechanisms: ↑HP, ↓OP, lymph obstruction, sodium/water retention, inflammation.
  • Exudate: inflammatory, protein/cell/fibrin rich, SG >1.020.
  • Transudate: pressure imbalance, protein/cell poor, SG <1.012.
  • CHF: back pressure + RAAS/ADH/SNS → edema.
  • Cirrhosis: portal HTN + hypoalbuminemia + sodium retention.
  • Nephrotic: proteinuria → hypoalbuminemia → ↓OP.
  • Pulmonary edema: pale pink alveolar fluid + septal congestion; dyspnea/orthopnea/PND.
  • Cerebral edema: narrowed sulci, widened gyri, herniation risk.

Hyperemia, congestion, hemorrhage

  • Hyperemia: active arteriolar dilation, bright red, oxygenated blood.
  • Congestion: passive venous outflow obstruction, dusky blue-red, acute or chronic.
  • Pulmonary CPC: fibrotic septa + heart failure cells = brown induration.
  • Nutmeg liver: dark centrilobular congestion, pale periportal sparing.
  • Hemorrhage: RBC extravasation after rupture.
  • Petechiae 1–2 mm; purpura >3 mm; ecchymosis 1–2 cm; hematoma = large pool.
  • Clinical effect depends on volume, rate, and site; pericardial/brain bleeding can be fatal at small volumes.

Hemostasis and thrombosis

  • Sequence: vasoconstriction → platelet plug → fibrin plug → antithrombotic control.
  • Adhesion: GPIb–vWF–collagen.
  • Dense granules: ADP, Ca²⁺, serotonin, histamine, epinephrine.
  • Alpha granules: fibrinogen, fibronectin, PDGF, PF4.
  • Common pathway: X → II to IIa → fibrinogen to fibrin → XIII stabilization.
  • Virchow: endothelial injury, abnormal flow, hypercoagulability.
  • Antemortem clot: attached, rough, Lines of Zahn.
  • Postmortem clot: nonadherent, smooth, perfect cast; chicken-fat/currant-jelly.

Thrombus morphology and DIC

  • Arterial thrombus: white, platelet/fibrin rich, occlusive.
  • Venous thrombus: red, RBC rich, stasis-associated, embolic risk.
  • Mural: cardiac chamber/aorta; vegetation: valve.
  • Fates: propagation, resolution, embolization, organization/recanalization.
  • DIC: systemic microthrombi → ischemia → factor consumption → hemorrhage → shock.
  • DIC associations: sepsis, burns, snake bites, shock, abruptio placentae, AFE, retained dead fetus, carcinomatosis.

Embolism

  • 90% of emboli are thromboemboli.
  • PTE source: 95% deep leg veins → IVC → RA → RV → PA.
  • Saddle embolus: main pulmonary artery bifurcation.
  • Paradoxical embolus: venous to arterial through septal defect.
  • Systemic emboli: usually left heart; lower extremities 75%, brain 10%.
  • Decompression: bends, chokes, cerebral effects; chronic avascular necrosis.
  • AFE: fetal squames/vernix/lanugo/meconium/mucus in pulmonary vessels; DIC/ARDS.
  • Fat embolism: long-bone fracture; empty spaces on H&E, red with Oil Red O/Sudan; syndrome = respiratory + neurologic + anemia + rash + thrombocytopenia.

Infarction and shock

  • Infarct: ischemic necrosis; usually wedge-shaped.
  • 99% thrombotic/thromboembolic arterial occlusion.
  • Coagulative necrosis in most organs; liquefactive in brain.
  • White infarct: solid end-arterial organs.
  • Red infarct: venous occlusion, loose tissue, dual supply, prior congestion, reperfusion.
  • Shock: systemic hypoperfusion from ↓CO or ↓effective circulating volume.
  • Stages: compensated → progressive acidosis → irreversible organ failure.
  • Shock organs: ischemic brain, contraction-band/subendocardial heart injury, ATN, ARDS/hyaline membranes, adrenal lipid depletion, hemorrhagic enteropathy, central liver necrosis.

Fast “if you see X, think Y” cues

If you see…Think…
Pale pink, relatively acellular alveolar materialPulmonary edema / transudate
Hemosiderin-laden alveolar macrophagesHeart failure cells / chronic passive congestion
Alternating pale and dark layers in intravascular massLines of Zahn / antemortem thrombus
Long red thrombus at PA bifurcationSaddle pulmonary thromboembolus
Fetal squames in pulmonary microvessels postpartumAmniotic fluid embolism
Round empty intravascular spaces after long-bone fractureFat embolism
Pale wedge in spleen or kidneyWhite/anemic infarct with coagulative necrosis
Dark red infarct in lungHemorrhagic infarct due to loose tissue/dual supply
Microthrombi plus bleedingDIC / consumption coagulopathy
Hypotension + oliguria + acidosisProgressive shock

Content integrated exclusively from the uploaded Pathology Lecture Transes 6–8 and Pathology Laboratory trans.
